structured.description: A Camera class that handles view and projection matrices for rendering 3D graphics. The class has abstract methods for calculating these matrices and an instance method for getting the view and projection matrices. Additionally, there are inner classes for handling transformation and translation of the camera. Overall, the code provides a way to manipulate the view and projection matrices of a 3D camera in Java.

items.description: Is an abstract class that provides methods for calculating and manipulating matrices related to camera transformations and viewport adjustments. It has fields for projection, viewProjectionMat4, values, transform, and a method for calculateViewMatrix. Additionally, it has abstract methods for calculateProjectionMatrix and adjustToViewport.
items.name: Camera

items.description: Retrieves a matrix object representing the view projection transformation, which combines the camera's view transform and projection. If the matrix is null or has changed, it calculates the view matrix using the `calculateViewMatrix()` method.

returns.description: a Matrix4f object representing the view projection matrix.

usage.code:
Camera camera = new Camera(projectionMatrix);
Matrix4f viewProjectionMat = camera.getViewProjection();
usage.description: In this example, a new instance of the Camera class is created with a projection matrix and then the getViewProjection method is called to retrieve the view projection matrix. The method returns a Matrix4f object that represents the concatenation of the camera's projection matrix and its rotation matrix.

items.name: getViewProjection

items.description: Calculates the view matrix by multiplying the camera's rotation and translation matrices using the projection matrix. The resulting matrix represents the transformation from world space to the camera's view.

returns.description: a 4x4 matrix representing the view transformation of a camera.

usage.description: In this example, the Camera class is initialized with a projection matrix and a transform component that contains the position and rotation of the camera. The calculateViewMatrix method is then called to generate the view matrix for the camera. This view matrix can be used in subsequent graphics rendering operations to position objects in the world relative to the camera's perspective.

items.name: calculateViewMatrix

items.description: Generates a matrix that represents the translation of a 3D transform from its original position to the opposite side of the screen.

returns.description: a 4x4 matrix representing the translation of the camera's position in homogeneous coordinates.

items.name: getTranslationMatrix

items.description: Retrieves the `transform` object and returns it, allowing for easy access to the transformation capabilities of the class.

returns.description: a reference to an instance of the `Transform` class.

usage.description: In this example, we create a new instance of the `Camera` class and use the `getTransform()` method to retrieve the transform object associated with it. The returned `myTrans` object can be used to perform transformations on the camera position and rotation.

items.name: getTransform

items.description: Is an abstract class that serves as a base for other classes in the "Camera" package. It contains an abstract method called getAsMatrix4() and does not have any fields or methods of its own. The purpose of this class is to provide a common base for other classes in the package, allowing them to inherit its properties and methods without having to duplicate code.
items.name: CameraStruct

structured.description: A 3D camera class called Camera3D, which inherits from the Camera class and provides additional functionality for handling input and calculating projections. The class has several methods for adjusting the viewport, calculating view and projection matrices, and processing input events such as mouse movements and keyboard key presses. The code also includes a inner class called CameraStruct3D that represents the camera's state, including its field of view, aspect ratio, near and far distances, and rotation.

items.description: Defines a camera class that extends the Camera class and provides additional functionality for a 3D environment. The class has a constructor that takes fov, aspect, zNear, and zFar parameters to calculate the projection matrix, and an adjustToViewport method to set the camera's viewport size. The class also has a processInput method that processes keyboard input and moves the camera accordingly.
items.name: Camera3D

items.description: Calculates a matrix representing the projection of a 3D scene onto a 2D view plane, based on input data provided by the `CameraStruct` object.

params.name: data
params.description: 3D camera's intrinsic and extrinsic parameters, which are used to calculate the projection matrix.

returns.description: a `Matrix4f` object representing the projection matrix.

items.name: calculateProjectionMatrix

items.description: Adjusts the camera's projection and view matrices to fit within the bounds of a specified viewport. It sets the aspect ratio of the camera's projection matrix based on the width and height of the viewport, calculates the view matrix, and sets the viewport dimensions using `GL11.glViewport()`.

params.name: width
params.description: 2D viewport width in pixels for which the camera's projection and view matrices are calculated and displayed.

params.name: height
params.description: 2D viewport dimension of the screen, which is used to calculate the projection and view matrices for rendering the 3D scene in the appropriate scale.

usage.description: The example would be used as shown below:

items.name: adjustToViewport

items.description: Is an extension of the CameraStruct class and provides additional functionality for representing a 3D camera perspective. It contains fields for fov, aspect, zNear, and zFar, which are used to calculate the matrix for perspectives and viewport dimensions. The getAsMatrix4() method returns a Matrix4f object initialized with the camera's perspective parameters.
items.name: CameraStruct3D

items.description: Initializes a matrix representing a perspective projection with the specified field of view (fov), aspect ratio, near and far distances.

returns.description: a matrix representation of a perspective projection, initialized with the specified field of view, aspect ratio, near and far distances.

items.name: getAsMatrix4

items.description: Processes mouse and keyboard input to move an object in a 3D space. It rotates the object based on user input, and accelerates or decelerates its movement based on the shift key press.

params.name: dt
params.description: time step, which is used to calculate the movement of the object based on its velocity and sensitivity.

params.name: speed
params.description: 3D movement speed of the object being controlled, which is multiplied by the time step `dt` to determine the total movement distance.

params.name: sens
params.description: sensitivity of the character's movement in response to user input, which determines how much the character will move based on small changes in user input.

usage.description: The method processInput is called with the inputs 'dt' and 'speed', which are both floats. The value of dt represents the time elapsed since last frame, while speed determines how fast the camera moves. Sensitivity controls the rate at which the camera rotates in response to user input.

items.name: processInput

items.description: Moves an object by a specified distance in a specified direction, applying the transformation of the object's parent transform.

params.name: dir
params.description: 3D direction to move the object in the specified amount, which is calculated as the product of the `dir` vector and the scaling factor `amt`.

params.name: amt
params.description: amount of movement to apply to the object's position along the specified direction.

items.name: move

structured.description: A Matrix4f class that can be used for various mathematical operations involving 3D matrices. It provides several constructors to create matrices with different properties, such as identity matrix, translation matrix, rotation matrix, and orthographic projection matrix. Additionally, it provides methods to transform vectors using the matrix, get the matrix data as an array of floats, and transpose the matrix. The class uses Java's built-in packages for mathematical operations.

items.description: Is a 4x4 matrix representation class that provides various methods for manipulating and transforming matrices. It has several methods such as initRotation, transform, mul, getData, getLinearData, get, set, transposeSelf, etc. These methods allow users to perform various operations on the matrix, including rotation, translation, scaling, and more. The class also provides a data array that stores the matrix elements, which can be accessed through the getData() method.
items.name: Matrix4f

items.description: Initializes a Matrix4f object to the identity matrix, setting all elements to their default values.

returns.description:
a Matrix4f object with identity matrix values.

The `Matrix4f` object is initialized to have an identity matrix, with all elements set to either 0 or 1. Specifically, the elements in the first row and column are set to [1, 0, 0, 0], and the remaining elements are set to [0, 1, 0, 0]. This means that when this matrix is multiplied by any other matrix, it will result in a matrix with the same elements as the original matrix.

usage.code:
Matrix4f myMatrix = new Matrix4f();
myMatrix.initIdentity(); // This sets the matrix to the identity matrix.
usage.description:
Here, we create a new instance of Matrix4f and then use the initIdentity() method to set it to the identity matrix. 
This is useful for cases where you want to initialize your matrix with the values that represent the identity matrix. It would be useful in the case where you wanted to reset your matrix back to its default state.  
Here are some examples of how we might use this method:

items.name: initIdentity

items.description: Initializes a matrix with translation coordinates, performing simple transformations to the x, y, and z components of the matrix.

params.name: x
params.description: 3D translation component along the x-axis in the generated matrix.

params.name: y
params.description: 2D translation of the matrix in the y-axis direction, which is added to the previous value stored in the matrix at position (1, 0).

params.name: z
params.description: 3rd component of the translation vector, which is added to the matrix's second row.

returns.description:
a reference to the original matrix.

* The `Matrix4f` object is returned, which represents a 4x4 homogeneous transformation matrix.
* The matrix elements have been updated to reflect the translation (x, y, z) applied to the origin of the coordinate system. Specifically, the x, y, and z components of each element in the matrix have been modified accordingly.
* The matrix remains invertible, meaning it can be used to transform vectors or points in 3D space.

usage.description: This code will create a matrix 'm' and initialize it with the values for the translation by (1, 2, 3). The values in this case are the same as those used to set the default values of the matrix, so the resulting matrix is the identity matrix.

items.name: initTranslation

items.description: Initializes a rotation matrix based on three Euler angles (x, y, z) and returns the transformed matrix.

params.name: x
params.description: 3D rotation around the x-axis and is used to calculate the z-component of the resulting rotation matrix.

params.name: y
params.description: 2D rotation angle about the x-axis, which is used to compute the 3D rotation matrix `ry`.

params.name: z
params.description: 3D rotation axis around which the rotation is performed, and it is used to calculate the rotation matrix `rz`.

returns.description:
a new Matrix4f object representing the rotation matrix.

* The `data` field contains the transformed rotation matrix. It is a 4x4 matrix representing a rotation in 3D space.
* The matrix elements represent the rotations around the x, y, and z axes, respectively. Each element is a complex number representing the sin and cos values of the corresponding angle.

usage.code:
Matrix4f rotation = new Matrix4f();
rotation.initRotation(0, 0, 0); // All rotations are 0 degrees in this case
usage.description: This would result in a matrix with no rotation. The values of the matrix would be:

items.name: initRotation

items.description: Sets the scale factors for the matrix's elements, assigning new values to the matrix's data array and returning a reference to the modified matrix.

params.name: x
params.description: 3D translation amount along the x-axis when initializing the matrix.

params.name: y
params.description: 2D scaling factor for the matrix in the `initScale()` function.

params.name: z
params.description: 2nd dimension of the scale factor in the matrix, which is used to transform the coordinate values in the 2D plane.

returns.description:
a reference to the original matrix.

The `Matrix4f` object is returned unchanged.

The input parameters `x`, `y`, and `z` are assigned to the respective elements of the matrix's data array.

The matrix's data array has four rows and four columns, with each element representing a 3D coordinate in the form of (x, y, z).

usage.code: Matrix4f matrix = new Matrix4f().initScale(2, 3, 5);
usage.description:
In this example, the scale values are set to x=2, y=3, z=5. The resulting matrix will scale every vector by these amounts in their respective directions. For example, if a vector (x,y,z) is multiplied by this matrix, it would result in the vector (2*x, 3*y, 5*z).
It is important to note that while the values are all floats, they represent specific scaling operations. The 'x' value scales the x direction of a vector by 2.0, and so on for y and z directions. If a matrix was initialized with the following values:

items.name: initScale

items.description: Initializes a matrix for a perspective projection, setting elements to define the field of view, aspect ratio, near and far plane distances, and other parameters needed for perspective transformation.

params.name: fov
params.description: field of view, which determines the angle of the perspective projection and is used to calculate the tan(halfFOV) value needed for the matrix initialization.

params.name: aspectRatio
params.description: 2D screen aspect ratio of the viewport, which determines the scaling factor for the x- and y-axes when projecting the 3D space onto the 2D image.

params.name: zNear
params.description: near clipping plane distance in the perspective projection, which determines the range of values that are transformed from the original coordinate system to the view-dependent coordinate system.

params.name: zFar
params.description: 4th coordinate of the projection matrix, which is used to calculate the height of objects in the virtual environment.

returns.description:
a `Matrix4f` object with values that define a perspective projection matrix.

* The `data` array has 4 rows and 16 columns, representing the 4D homogeneous coordinates of a perspective projection matrix.
* Each column represents a component of the projection matrix, with the first three components representing the x, y, and z positions of the projection, respectively, and the last component representing the w component, which is the weight for the perspective transformation.
* The values in each row are the coefficients of the perspective projection matrix, which are computed based on the input fov, aspectRatio, zNear, and zFar parameters.

usage.code:
Matrix4f proj = new Matrix4f();
proj.initPerspective(70, 16 / 9, 0.1f, 100);
usage.description:
This code creates a matrix with perspective projection that has a field of view of 70 degrees, an aspect ratio of 16/9, a near plane distance of 0.1 and a far plane distance of 100. The resulting projection matrix will be used in conjunction with other transformations to create a 3D scene.

The method initPerspective takes four arguments: fov (field of view), aspectRatio (a measure of the screen's width/height ratio), zNear (the distance from the eye to the near clipping plane) and zFar (the distance from the eye to the far clipping plane). The resulting projection matrix is stored in the Matrix4f object. 

The first thing we do is import the necessary class that represents a 3D matrix, which is the Matrix4f class. We then create an instance of this class and call the initPerspective method with some arguments to initialize it. In this case, the fov argument is set to 70 degrees (the same as the field of view in the example code), aspect ratio is set to 16/9 (which is a common value for screen ratios) and zNear and zFar are set to 0.1 and 100 respectively, which correspond to a near clipping plane at 0.1 units away from the eye and a far clipping plane at 100 units away from the eye (the far plane is not actually used in this case as we do not want to perform any perspective correction).

Once we have called the initPerspective method, we have created a projection matrix that represents our perspective transformation. This can be used to create a 3D scene by multiplying it with other matrices that represent transformations such as translations, rotations and scale factors.

items.name: initPerspective

items.description: Sets up an orthographic projection matrix with given dimensions, aspect ratio, and near and far clipping planes.

params.name: left
params.description: left coordinate of the orthographic projection, which is used to scale the matrix's rows and columns.

params.name: right
params.description: right side of the orthographic projection, which is used to calculate the coordinates of the pixels in the resulting image.

params.name: bottom
params.description: 2D coordinate of the bottom-left corner of the orthographic projection, which is used to determine the dimensions of the projection matrix.

params.name: top
params.description: 2D coordinate of the upper-left corner of the view frustum, which is used to calculate the width and height of the frustum in the formula for `data[1][3]`.

params.name: near
params.description: near clipping plane of the orthographic projection, which determines the distance from the viewer at which objects appear to be in front of or behind the near plane.

params.name: far
params.description: 3D far clip plane distance, which determines how far away objects can be rendered in the orthogonal projection.

returns.description:
a matrix representing an orthographic projection of a 3D space.

* The matrix is a 4x4 Orthographic projection matrix.
* The data array contains the values for each element of the matrix, with the first three elements representing the x, y, and z scaling factors, and the last three elements representing the x, y, and z translation vectors.
* The matrix will transform points in 3D space from an orthographic viewpoint, where the near and far clipping planes are defined by the `near` and `far` parameters, respectively.

usage.description: In this example we have a matrix named m which is initialized by calling the initOrthographic method with the given arguments.

items.name: initOrthographic

items.description: Initializes a rotation matrix based on two input vectors: `forward` and `up`. It computes the cross product of these vectors to obtain the rotation axis and then sets the rotation angle using the resulting vector. The rotation angle is then applied to create a rotation matrix that can be used for 3D transformations.

params.name: forward
params.description: 3D direction of the rotation axis.

params.name: up
params.description: 3D direction perpendicular to the forward direction, which is used to calculate the rotation matrix.

returns.description: a Matrix4f object representing a rotation matrix based on the input vectors.

usage.description:
In this example, the forward vector and the up vector are both normalized, which means that they have a length of one. This allows us to use the cross product between them to create the right vector, which is the vector perpendicular to the other two vectors. Then, we can use the cross product again to get the other two vectors, flipping the order this time.

We then call initRotation() on the rotation matrix and pass in the normalized forward and up vectors. This method will return a rotation matrix that rotates a vector counter-clockwise around the given axis by an angle of 90 degrees (pi/2 radians).

items.name: initRotation

items.description: Sets the rotation matrix based on three input vectors: `forward`, `right`, and `up`. It returns the updated rotation matrix.

params.name: forward
params.description:
3D direction of rotation, which is used to initialize the x, y, and z components of the rotation matrix.

* `forward` is a 3D vector representing the direction of forward motion.

params.name: up
params.description:
3D vector that points upwards from the rotation axis, which is used to determine the rotation matrix's vertical component.

* `up` is a vector representing the upward direction in the 3D space.

params.name: right
params.description:
3D right vector of the rotation axis, which is used to initialize the components of the matrix.

* `right`: A vector representing the rightward direction of the rotation. Its magnitude is typically non-zero, indicating that the rotation is not purely around the x, y, or z axis.

returns.description:
a `Matrix4f` object representing the rotation matrix.

The output is a `Matrix4f` object, which represents a 4x4 homogeneous transformation matrix.

The elements of the matrix are set based on the input vectors `forward`, `up`, and `right`. Specifically, the elements in the first two rows represent the rotation components around the x, y, and z axes, respectively, while the elements in the third row represent the translation component along the fourth axis. The element in the fourth row is always 1, indicating that the matrix is a full rotation matrix.

usage.description:
The resulting rotation matrix is used to transform objects in a scene and rotate them around their axes. The forward vector indicates which way the object will move when it is moved forward, the up vector determines how the object will rotate around its X axis, and the right vector specifies how the object will rotate around its Y axis.

This code creates a new rotation matrix 'm' using the vectors f, u, and r to initialize its values. The method initRotation takes these three vectors as arguments and sets them as the forward, up, and right vectors in the resulting matrix.

items.name: initRotation

items.description: Takes a `Vector3f` argument `r` and returns a new `Vector3f` object with the result of multiplying the components of the input vector by the corresponding components of a set of data values, stored in an array.

params.name: r
params.description: 4D vector that is multiplied element-wise with the 3D vector returned by the function.

returns.description: a new Vector3f object containing the result of multiplying each element of the input Vector3f object by the corresponding elements of a set of data, followed by addition of the resultant values.

usage.description: The output of this example would be (1, 4, 9).

items.name: transform

items.description: Multiplies two matrix objects and returns the result as a new matrix object.

params.name: r
params.description:
4x4 matrix to be multiplied with the current matrix.

* It is an instance of the `Matrix4f` class.
* It represents a 4x4 homogeneous matrix.

returns.description:
a new Matrix4f object containing the result of multiplying the input matrix by another matrix.

* The `res` variable is initialized as an instance of `Matrix4f`.
* The values of the input matrices `data` and `r` are multiplied element-wise to produce the output.
* Each element of the output matrix is calculated by multiplying the corresponding elements of `data` and `r`, using the indexing mechanism provided by the matrix structure.

usage.description:
In this example, we create two matrices a and b by initializing them with the initIdentity() method and setting their scales and translations using the setScale() and setTranslation() methods respectively.  Then we multiply these matrices together using the mul() method and print out the result.  
This is how you would use this method if you wanted to transform a vector by two different matrices.

items.name: mul

items.description: Returns an array of arrays, each element being a copy of the original array `data`.

returns.description: an array of arrays, where each inner array contains four elements representing the input data.

usage.description:
In this example, a new instance of the Matrix4f class is created and then the getData method is called on it. The result of calling this method is assigned to a 2D array of type float.
The 2D array returned from the getData method contains four rows and four columns where each element in the matrix is stored as a float value. Therefore, the return type of the getData method is 2D float array.

items.name: getData

items.description: Returns an array of float values, representing a linear data set with nine data points in two dimensions.

returns.description: an array of 12 floating-point values.

usage.description: This code creates a new Matrix4f object and then uses the getLinearData() method to create an array representing the matrix in a linearized format. It then prints the contents of this array using the toString() method from the Java Arrays class. The output should be 16 numbers that represent the values in the linearized array representation of the Matrix4f object.

items.name: getLinearData

items.description: Retrieves a value from a 2D array `data`. The value is located at position (x, y) and returned as a float.

params.name: x
params.description: 1D coordinate of the point in the data array to be retrieved.

params.name: y
params.description: 2nd dimension of the data array being accessed by the function.

returns.description: a floating-point value representing the element at position (x, y) of a 2D array.

usage.code: float x = matrix.get(0, 1);
usage.description: In this case, the value returned by the method get for the first argument (x) and the second argument (y) would be equal to 0 as the first row of a matrix has values in it's columns starting from the top left corner.

items.name: get

items.description: Sets the value of a private field `data` to a provided `float[][]` argument.

params.name: data
params.description: 2D array of float values that will be stored in the `data` field of the calling object.

usage.description: This code creates a 4x4 float array 'data' that serves as the input for the SetM() method, and then uses this input to initialize a Matrix4f object using the SetM() method. The resulting matrix m will have its data member equal to the float array 'data'.

items.name: SetM

items.description: Sets a value at a specific position in a two-dimensional array of integers and floats.

params.name: x
params.description: 0-based index of a cell in the 2D array `data`.

params.name: y
params.description: 2nd dimension of the data array being manipulated, and it determines which element in the array is being assigned the `value` parameter.

params.name: value
params.description: 3D coordinate value that will be assigned to the corresponding cell in the `data` array.

usage.code:
Matrix4f matrix = new Matrix4f();
matrix.set(1, 2, 3);
usage.description: In this example the value 3 is being assigned to the index position (1, 2) of a matrix object, where x and y are integers that represent the column and row position respectively in the 2D array representing the matrix.

items.name: set

items.description: Transforms an array of arrays into a new array by swapping elements between rows and columns.

usage.description:
This code will initialize a matrix with the identity matrix and then transpose it. The resulting matrix will be the same as the identity matrix.
It is important to note that the method initRotation can also be used to rotate the matrix around any axis. If this happens, the output matrix will not be the same as the original matrix.

items.name: transposeSelf

structured.description: A `Vector2f` class that provides various methods for manipulating and transforming 2D vectors. These methods include length, maximum value, dot product, normalization, cross product, lerping, rotating, adding and subtracting other vectors or floating values, multiplying with other vectors, dividing by another vector or a scalar value, as well as providing an absolute value and setting individual components of the vector. Additionally, the class provides methods for converting the vector to a 3D vector and checking for equality with other vectors.

items.description: Is a mathematical representation of a 2D vector with x and y components. It provides various methods for calculating and manipulating the vector's length, direction, and angle, as well as methods for comparing vectors and converting between different representations of the vector. Additionally, it includes high-level methods for rotating, scaling, and adding/subtracting/multiplying vectors.
items.name: Vector2f

items.description: Calculates the Euclidean distance between a point and the origin, represented by (0, 0).

returns.description: the square root of the sum of the squares of the `x` and `y` coordinates.

usage.code:
Vector2f vector = new Vector2f(3, 4);
float distance = vector.length(); // output will be 5
usage.description: In this example, we create a Vector2f object with x = 3 and y = 4 and then call the length method on it to get its distance from (0, 0). The output is 5 as expected.

items.name: length

items.description: Calculates the maximum value of two floating-point numbers `x` and `y`. It returns the larger of the two values.

returns.description: the larger of `x` and `y`.

items.name: max

items.description: Calculates the dot product of a `Vector2f` instance `r` and the component-wise multiplication of its `x` and `y` values with those of the input `Vector2f`.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector that provides the dot product value when multiplied by the `x` and `y` components of the function's output.

returns.description: a scalar value representing the dot product of the input vector and another vector.

items.name: dot

items.description: Normalizes a given vector by dividing its components by the vector's magnitude, returning a new vector with normalized coordinates.

returns.description: a normalized vector with x and y components scaled by the length of the original vector.

usage.description: In this example, the method normalized is called on an instance of Vector2f with coordinates (3,4). The normalized method returns a new Vector2f object with the same direction as the original vector, but with a length of 1. This means that the output of the getX() and getY() methods will be the coordinates of the normalized vector, which is (0.6, 0.8).

items.name: normalized

items.description: Computes the dot product of two vectors and returns the result as a scalar value.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector to be crossed with the current vector.

returns.description: a floating-point value representing the cross product of two vectors.

items.name: cross

items.description: Computes a vector interpolation between two input vectors, scaling the difference between them by the given factor and adding the result to the starting vector.

params.name: dest
params.description: 2D destination vector to which the current vector is interpolated.

params.name: lerpFactor
params.description: factor by which the current position is to be linearly interpolated towards the destination position.

returns.description: a new `Vector2f` object representing the interpolated value between the original vector and the destination vector.

usage.description:
In this example, we start with two vectors (v1 and v2), which both have coordinates (0, 0) and (5, 6), respectively. We define a third vector (result), which is used to store the result of the lerp method call. The lerpFactor variable is set to 0.5f, which represents how much the destination vector should be used in the calculation.

The code then calls the lerp method on v1, passing in v2 and the lerpFactor value as arguments. This will calculate a new vector that is a linear combination of v1 and v2, using the specified lerp factor. The result of this method call is assigned to the result variable.

The resulting vector (result) will have coordinates that are 50% of the way between the coordinates of v1 and v2. In other words, it will have a coordinate of (2.5f, 3.5f).

items.name: lerp

items.description: Takes a float argument representing the angle of rotation in radians and returns a new vector with its x and y components rotated accordingly.

params.name: angle
params.description: angle of rotation in radians, which is used to calculate the x and y components of the returned vector.

returns.description: a vector with x and y components that have been rotated by the specified angle.

items.name: rotate

items.description: Calculates and returns a new `Vector2f` object with the sum of its own `x` and `y` components and those of the provided `r` argument.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector to be added to the current vector.

returns.description: a new `Vector2f` object representing the sum of the input vectors.

items.name: add

items.description: Adds a scalar value to the vector's x and y components, returning a new vector with the updated coordinates.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector that is added to the existing vector.

returns.description: a new `Vector2f` instance with the sum of the current vector's x and y coordinates and the input value added to it.

items.name: add

items.description: Takes two floating-point values `x` and `y`, calculates their sum and returns a new `Vector2f` object containing the result.

params.name: x
params.description: 2D coordinate to be added to the current vector's position.

params.name: y
params.description: 2nd component of the resulting vector.

returns.description: a new `Vector2f` instance representing the sum of the input `x` and `y` values.

usage.description: In this example, the vector v1 is added to the vector v2 and stored in the variable `result`. The output of the program would be "The result is (8 10)", which is the sum of the two vectors.

items.name: add

items.description: Calculates the vector difference between the current vector and a provided reference vector, returning a new vector object.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector that the method will subtract from the input `Vector2f`.

returns.description: a new `Vector2f` instance representing the difference between the input vector and the reference vector.

items.name: sub

items.description: Subtracts a `Vector2f` component from the input vector, resulting in a new vector with the difference between the input and the specified component.

params.name: r
params.description: offset that is subtracted from the vector's `x` and `y` components to produce the resultant vector.

returns.description: a new `Vector2f` instance representing the difference between the original vector and the given value.

items.name: sub

items.description: Multiplies a `Vector2f` object by another `Vector2f` object, returning a new `Vector2f` object with the product of the two component values.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector to be multiplied with the current vector.

returns.description: a new vector with the product of the input vector's x and y components multiplied by the input parameter vector's x and y components.

items.name: mul

items.description: Takes a scalar value `r` and returns a new `Vector2f` instance with the product of the existing vector's x-component and `r`, and the same y-component.

params.name: r
params.description: scalar value that multiplies the x and y components of the `Vector2f` instance being manipulated.

returns.description: a vector with components scaled by the given scalar value.

usage.code:
Vector2f vector = new Vector2f(3, 4);
vector.mul(5); //Returns a new vector with the x and y coordinates multiplied by 5.

items.name: mul

items.description: Takes a `Vector2f` argument `r` and returns a new `Vector2f` instance with x-coordinate calculated as `x / r.getX()` and y-coordinate calculated as `y / r.getY()`.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector to divide by, and its value is used to calculate the output vector's x and y components.

returns.description: a vector with the same x-coordinate as the input vector, scaled by the reciprocal of the input vector's x-coordinate.

items.name: div

items.description: Multiplies its input by a scalar value and returns a new `Vector2f` instance with scaled x and y components.

params.name: r
params.description: scalar value used to divide the vector's components by.

returns.description: a vector with scaled X and Y components proportional to the input parameter `r`.

usage.description: Explanation: The method div is called on the vector myVector and takes in a float parameter divisor, which represents the value to divide all coordinates of the vector by. In this example, the vector's original x and y values are divided by 5, resulting in new coordinates (x=2, y=2).

items.name: div

items.description: Computes and returns a new `Vector2f` instance with the absolute values of its x and y components.

returns.description: a new `Vector2f` object containing the absolute values of the input vector's `x` and `y` components.

usage.code:
Vector2f v = new Vector2f(-1, -2);
v.abs(); // returns a new vector with x=1 and y=2

items.name: abs

items.description: Returns a string representation of a point in the form "(x,y)".

returns.description: a string representation of the object's state, consisting of two values separated by a space.

usage.code:
Vector2f v = new Vector2f(1, 2);
System.out.println(v.toString()); // Outputs "(1 2)"

items.name: toString

items.description: Modifies the object's `x` and `y` fields to reflect the input values, returning a reference to the modified object.

params.name: x
params.description: 2D coordinate value to assign to the `x` component of the `Vector2f` object.

params.name: y
params.description: 2nd component of the `Vector2f` object being modified, and its value is assigned to the `y` field of the object.

returns.description: a reference to the modified `Vector2f` instance.

usage.code:
Vector2f vector = new Vector2f(0, 0);
vector.set(-1, -1); // Sets the vector's x and y values to -1 and -1 respectively.

items.name: set

items.description: Sets the x and y components of the vector to the corresponding values of the provided reference `r`.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector to be set as the value of the method's return, which is a reference to an instance of the `Vector2f` class.

returns.description: a reference to the original `Vector2f` object, unchanged.

items.name: set

items.description: Converts a vector of type `float[]` into a `Vector3f` object, returning a new instance with the same components but with the `z`-component set to zero.

returns.description: a new `Vector3f` object containing the x, y, and z coordinates of the input vector.

items.name: as3DVector

items.description: Retrieves the value of the `x` field.

returns.description: a floating-point value representing the x coordinate.

usage.code:
Vector2f myVector = new Vector2f(3, 4);
float xCoord = myVector.getX(); //xCoord will equal 3.0

items.name: getX

items.description: Sets the value of the `x` field to the input float value provided in the argument list.

params.name: x
params.description: float value that will be assigned to the `x` field of the class instance being manipulated by the function.

usage.code:
Vector2f vector = new Vector2f(0, 0); // create a vector at the origin
vector.setX(5.3);  // change the x-coordinate of the vector to 5.3
System.out.println(vector);  // prints (5.3, 0)

items.name: setX

items.description: Returns the value of its `y` field.

returns.description: the value of the `y` field.

items.name: getY

items.description: Sets the value of the field `y` to the argument passed as a float.

params.name: y
params.description: float value to be assigned to the `y` field of the class instance being manipulated by the `setY()` method.

usage.code:
Vector2f myVector = new Vector2f();
myVector.setY(5.0f); // Sets the y-coordinate of the vector to 5.0f

items.name: setY

items.description: Compares a `Vector2f` instance with another provided as parameter, returning `true` if both x and y components are equal, otherwise `false`.

params.name: r
params.description: 2D vector to be compared with the current vector.

returns.description: a boolean value indicating whether the vector's `x` and `y` components are equal to those of the provided vector.

usage.description: In this example, we create two `Vector2f` objects with different coordinates and then use the equals method to compare them. Since the coordinates of the two vectors are not the same, the method returns `false`.

items.name: equals

structured.description: A `Vector3f` class, which represents a 3D vector in the homogeneous coordinate form (x, y, z, w). The class provides various methods for manipulating vectors, including addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division by other vectors or scalars. It also includes methods for calculating the vector's length, square length, maximum value, dot product, cross product, normalization, rotation, and lerp. Additionally, the code defines several instance methods for setting and getting the vector's components, as well as providing methods for checking equality with other vectors.

items.description: Is a mathematical representation of a three-dimensional point in space. It has fields for x, y, and z coordinates, as well as methods for basic arithmetic operations, rotation, and scaling. The class also provides methods for computing the magnitude, direction, and normalization of the vector, as well as setting and getting the coordinates.
items.name: Vector3f

items.description: Calculates the Euclidean distance of a 3D point from the origin, using the square root of the sum of the squared x, y, and z coordinates.

returns.description: the square root of the sum of the squares of the coordinates of a 3D point.

usage.code:
Vector3f v = new Vector3f(3, 4, 5);
float distance = v.length(); // returns 5.0

items.name: length

items.description: Calculates the length of a point in 3D space by squaring its coordinates and summing them.

returns.description: a value representing the square of the length of a vector in 3D space.

items.name: squareLength

items.description: Computes and returns the maximum value among `x`, `y`, and `z`.

returns.description: the maximum value of `x`, `y`, and `z`.

items.name: max

items.description: Computes the dot product of a `Vector3f` object and another vector, returning the result as a float value.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to be dot-producted with the `x`, `y`, and `z` components of the function's output.

returns.description: a scalar value representing the dot product of the input vector and the vector represented by the `x`, `y`, and `z` variables.

items.name: dot

items.description: Computes and returns the cross product of two vectors in 3D space, represented as `Vector3f` objects.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to be crossed with the current vector, resulting in a new 3D vector.

returns.description: a new vector with the cross product of the input vectors.

items.name: cross

items.description: Normalizes a 3D vector by dividing its components by the magnitude of the vector, resulting in a unit-length vector.

returns.description: a normalized vector in the form of a `Vector3f`.

usage.code:
Vector3f vector = new Vector3f(1, 2, 3);
Vector3f normalized = vector.normalized();
usage.description: In this example, the normalize() method takes a Vector3f object and returns a new Vector3f object that is a unit vector in the same direction as the original vector. The length of the original vector is calculated using the `length()` method and used to scale the x, y, and z components of the original vector to produce the normalized vector.

items.name: normalized

items.description: Rotates a vector by an angle around a specified axis, returning the new vector.

params.name: axis
params.description: 3D vector that defines the rotation axis.

params.name: angle
params.description: 3D rotation angle about the specified `axis`.

returns.description: a rotated vector based on the given axis and angle of rotation.

usage.description: This will output:

items.name: rotate

items.description: Takes a quaternion argument `rotation`, multiplies it with the current vector's position, and returns the rotated vector's position.

params.name: rotation
params.description: 3D rotation transformation to be applied to the Vector3f object.

returns.description: a vector representing the rotated position of the original vector.

items.name: rotate

items.description: Calculates a linear interpolation between two vectors, resulting in a third vector that is a weighted combination of the original inputs.

params.name: dest
params.description: 3D vector to which the lerped value will be added.

params.name: lerpFactor
params.description: 0.0-1.0 factor by which the current position is to be interpolated towards the destination position.

returns.description: a vector that interpolates between two given vectors, using a factor to control the interpolation amount.

items.name: lerp

items.description: Takes a `Vector3f` argument and returns a new `Vector3f` object with the sum of the current instance's coordinates and those of the input argument.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to be added to the current vector.

returns.description: a new `Vector3f` object representing the sum of the input vectors.

usage.description: In this example, we create two vectors with the x-y-z coordinates of (1, 2, 3) and (4, 5, 6) respectively, then use the add method to combine them into a new vector. The resulting vector has the coordinates (5, 7, 9).

items.name: add

items.description: Adds the components of a `Vector3f` object to the corresponding components of the current object.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to be added to the current position of the object, and its values are used to update the object's x, y, and z components.

items.name: addSelf

items.description: Adds a scalar value to a `Vector3f` object's components, returning a new `Vector3f` instance with the added value.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to be added to the current vector.

returns.description: a new `Vector3f` object with the sum of the original vector's components and the given float value.

items.name: add

items.description: Adds a vector to the current vector, scaling the input vector by the given scalar value before concatenation.

params.name: v
params.description: 3D vector to be scaled and added to the current vector.

params.name: scale
params.description: scalar value by which the input vector is multiplied before being added to the current vector.

returns.description: a new vector that is the result of adding the provided vector multiplied by a scale factor to the current vector.

items.name: addScaledVector

items.description: Multiplies a `Vector3f` object by a scalar value and adds it to the current vector, scaling the current vector accordingly.

params.name: v
params.description: 3D vector that will be scaled by the specified `scale` value before being added to the current vector.

params.name: scale
params.description: 3D scaling factor applied to the `Vector3f` object passed as argument to the function.

items.name: addSelfScaledVector

items.description: Takes a reference to another `Vector3f` object `r`, and returns a new `Vector3f` object representing the difference between the current object and `r`.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to be subtracted from the current vector, resulting in a new vector that represents the difference between the two.

returns.description: a new vector with the difference between the input vector and the current vector's components.

items.name: sub

items.description: Takes a single float argument `r` and returns a new `Vector3f` object representing the difference between the original vector and the `r` value subtracted from it.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D position to which the vector is being subtracted.

returns.description: a new vector representing the difference between the original vector and a given reference point.

items.name: sub

items.description: Takes a `Vector3f` object as input and returns a new `Vector3f` object with the dot product of the current vector and the given vector.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector that multiplies with the current vector, resulting in a new 3D vector output.

returns.description: a vector with the product of the input vectors' coordinates.

items.name: mul

items.description: Multiplies a vector by a scalar value, returning a new vector with the product.

params.name: r
params.description: scalar value that is multiplied with the vector components of the `Vector3f` object.

returns.description: a vector with x, y, and z components scaled by the input parameter `r`.

items.name: mul

items.description: Takes a reference to another vector and returns a new vector with the components scaled by the reciprocal of the corresponding component of the input vector.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to which the current vector is to be divided.

returns.description: a new vector with the same components as the input vector, scaled by the reciprocal of its magnitude.

items.name: div

items.description: Takes a scalar value `r` and returns a new `Vector3f` instance with components scaled by the ratio of the original vector's magnitude to `r`.

params.name: r
params.description: scalar value used to divide the vector components.

returns.description: a vector with the same x, y, and z values divided by the input `r`.

usage.description: In this example, we create a vector with the values (5, 6, 7) and divide it by the value 4 using the div method, resulting in a new vector with the values (5/4, 6/4, 7/4).

items.name: div

items.description: Computes and returns a vector with the absolute value of its components.

returns.description: a new vector with the absolute value of the input vector's components.

usage.description: The method abs is used to find the absolute value of each element in the vector. The output is a new vector with the absolute values of x, y and z.

items.name: abs

items.description: Returns a string representation of a mathematical expression, consisting of three variables: `x`, `y`, and `z`.

returns.description: a string representation of the current state of the object, including its `x`, `y`, and `z` properties.

items.name: toString

items.description: Returns a `Vector2f` object representing the coordinates (x, y) of some entity.

returns.description: a Vector2f object containing the x and y coordinates.

items.name: getXY

items.description: Returns a `Vector2f` object representing the y-axis and z-axis coordinates of a point.

returns.description: a `Vector2f` object containing the `y` and `z` coordinates of a point.

items.name: getYZ

items.description: Returns a `Vector2f` object representing the position (x and z) of an entity.

returns.description: a `Vector2f` object representing the point (x, z).

items.name: getZX

items.description: Returns a `Vector2f` instance representing the position (x, y) of an object.

returns.description: a `Vector2f` object representing the coordinates (x, y) of a point.

items.name: getYX

items.description: Returns a `Vector2f` object representing the point (z,y) coordinates.

returns.description: a `Vector2f` object representing the z-coordinate and y-coordinate of a point.

usage.code:
Vector3f myVector = new Vector3f(1, 2, 3);
Vector2f zy = myVector.getZY(); // will return (3, 2)

items.name: getZY

items.description: Returns a `Vector2f` object representing the x and z coordinates of a point.

returns.description: a `Vector2f` object containing the `x` and `z` coordinates of the point.

items.name: getXZ

items.description: Sets the coordinates of a `Vector3f` instance to the input values for `x`, `y`, and `z`.

params.name: x
params.description: 3D vector's x component, which is being set to the provided value.

params.name: y
params.description: 2D coordinate value that is being set for the `Vector3f` instance.

params.name: z
params.description: 3rd component of the vector, which is assigned the value provided by the user.

returns.description: a reference to the same `Vector3f` object, with its components set to the input values.

usage.description: This code will create a vector with the coordinates (0, 0, 0), and then change its x, y, and z values to 1, 2, and 3 using the set method. After setting the coordinates, the changed vector is then printed to the console, resulting in the output of (1, 2, 3).

items.name: set

items.description: Sets the components of a `Vector3f` object to the corresponding values of another `Vector3f` object.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector that sets the values of the `Vector3f` object.

returns.description: a reference to the original `Vector3f` object, unchanged.

items.name: set

items.description: Returns the value of the `x` field.

returns.description: a floating-point representation of the variable `x`.

items.name: getX

items.description: Sets the value of the `x` field of its object reference to the provided float value.

params.name: x
params.description: floating-point value that sets the `x` field of the class instance being modified by the function.

items.name: setX

items.description: Returns the value of the `y` field.

returns.description: the value of the `y` field.

items.name: getY

items.description: Sets the value of an object's `y` field to the input `float` value.

params.name: y
params.description: 𝑥 coordinate of an object, where the function is called to update or set that value.

items.name: setY

items.description: Retrieves the value of the `z` field.

returns.description: a floating-point value representing the z component of an object's position.

usage.code:
Vector3f vec = new Vector3f(); 
float zValue = vec.getZ(); // returns the value of the vector's z component, which is initially set to 0 in the constructor.
vec.setZ(10);   // sets the vector's z component to 10
zValue = vec.getZ(); // returns 10

items.name: getZ

items.description: Sets the value of the `z` field of the object to which it belongs.

params.name: z
params.description: 3D coordinates of an object's position in space, and it is being assigned the value passed as argument to the function.

items.name: setZ

items.description: Compares a `Vector3f` object with another object, returning `true` if all components are equal, and `false` otherwise.

params.name: r
params.description: 3D vector to compare with the current vector.

returns.description: a boolean value indicating whether the vector is equal to the provided reference vector.

items.name: equals

structured.description: A 3D graphics program that renders a blocky landscape with a camera and a shader. It uses various packages such as LWJGL, OpenGL, and Math. The code initializes display and GL settings, loads a model, texture, and camera, and updates the rendering loop. In the loop, it updates the view matrix based on user input and renders the scene using the shader.

items.description: Is a game engine that handles input, rendering, and world updates in a 3D environment. It uses the OpenGL API for rendering and has a basic camera and model loading functionality. The engine can be used to create a simple 3D game or application with basic rendering and physics capabilities.
items.name: Main

items.description: Initializes display and GL resources, enters an infinite loop, and exits with a successful return value of 0.

params.name: args
params.description: 1 or more command line arguments passed to the program when it is launched, which are used to initialize various components of the program.

items.name: main

items.description: Sets the display mode and creates a Display object with forward compatibility enabled, enables vertical synchronization, and prints the GPU version string using `GL11.glGetString()`.
items.name: initDisplay

items.description: Initializes various GL settings and creates a new instance of a `Camera3D`, `Texture`, and `Chunk` class, preparing for rendering a 3D world.
items.name: initGL

items.description: Continuously runs a loop while certain conditions are not met, updating and rendering the display, and displaying the current frame rate and memory usage.
items.name: loop

items.description: Updates the position of an object `w` based on input signals and transforms them using a matrix.

params.name: dt
params.description: 3D time step used to update the object's position and is multiplied by the input values to determine the update speed.

items.name: update

items.description: Performs rendering of 3D models using a scene graph and a camera. It sets up the rendering pipeline, including uniforms for color and transformation, and then draws the models using the model matrix.
items.name: render

items.description: Terminates the Java program with the specified exit status.

params.name: status
params.description: value to be passed to the `System.exit()` method, which terminates the Java application with the specified status code.

items.name: exit

structured.description: A `Model` class that loads and renders 3D models using the OpenGL API. The class takes in an array of vertices and an array of indices, which it uses to create a Vertex Array Object (VAO) and bind it with the appropriate vertex attributes. It then renders the model by drawing the vertices and indices using the `glDrawElements` method. Additionally, the code provides methods for enabling and disabling the vertex attributes, as well as getting the VAO handle and the number of vertices in the model.

items.description: Is used to handle vertex arrays and indices for rendering 3D models in a graphics application. It provides methods for drawing the model, enabling and disabling vertex attribs, getting the VAO and size of the model, and loading data from an array and indices.
items.name: Model

items.description: Binds a vertex array object, enables and disables vertex attributes for two attributes, and then calls either `glDrawArrays` or `glDrawElements` to render a triangle shape with specified vertices.

usage.code:
Model model = Model.load(vertices, indices);
model.draw();
usage.description: In this example, the vertices and indices are passed to the load method of the model class, which creates a VAO and stores the data in buffers. The draw method then binds the VAO, enables the vertex attributes, and renders the model using GL11.glDrawElements. Finally, it disables the vertex attributes and unbinds the VAO.

items.name: draw

items.description: Enables vertex attributes 0 and 1 for rendering.
items.name: enableAttribs

items.description: Disables both vertex attribute arrays.
items.name: disableAttribs

items.description: Retrieves the value of a field called `vao`.

returns.description: the value of `vao`, which is an `int`.

usage.code:
Model model = new Model();
int vaoID = model.getVAO();

items.name: getVAO

items.description: Returns the size of an object.

returns.description: the value of the `size` field.

usage.code:
Model model = new Model();
int size = model.getSize();

items.name: getSize

items.description: Loads a 3D model from a set of vertices and indices stored in arrays, creates a vertex array object (VAO), stores the indices and vertex data, unbinds the VAO, and returns a `Model` object representing the loaded model.

params.name: vertices
params.description: 3D model's geometry as a float array containing the coordinates of the vertices.

params.name: indices
params.description: 3D model's vertex positions in the format of an integer array, which is stored in the function for later use in rendering the model.

returns.description: a `Model` object representing the loaded 3D model.

items.name: load

items.description: Generates a Vertex Array Object (VAO) and binds it to the current context, allowing for efficient rendering of 3D models.

returns.description: an integer value representing a valid vertex array object (Vao) handle.

items.name: createVAO

items.description: Creates a vertex buffer object (VBO) to store floating-point data, binds it to the GPU, and sets up vertex attributes for rendering.

params.name: attrib
params.description: 2D vertex attribute index that corresponds to the data stored in the `data` array.

params.name: data
params.description: 2D array of floating-point values that will be stored in a vertex buffer object (VBO) and passed as attribute data to the GPU for rendering.

items.name: storeData

items.description: Genarates a buffer object for storing element array indices, binds it, and sets the data to be stored using `GL_STATIC_DRAW`.

params.name: indices
params.description: 3D model's indices to be stored in the buffer.

items.name: storeIndices

items.description: Terminates the binding of a vertex array object (VAO) by passing a zero value to `GL30.glBindVertexArray()`.
items.name: unbindVAO

structured.description: A class `Texture` that loads and manages textures for use in an OpenGL context. The class has several methods for binding and unbinding textures, as well as a constructor that takes a file path as input and loads the texture from the file using `ImageIO`. The loaded texture is then stored in an instance variable `id`.

items.description: Is used to load and manage textures in a 3D graphics application. It allows for loading textures from files, binding them to texture slots, and retrieving the ID of the loaded texture. The class also provides methods for flipping and manipulating the texture data.
items.name: Texture

items.description: Is a hook that executes when an object is about to be garbage collected. It provides a chance for objects to perform any necessary cleanup or resource management before they are reclaimed by the garbage collector.

usage.description: In the above example, the method finalize is used to release any resources held by the object Person when it is garbage collected.

items.name: finalize

items.description: 0 is invoked, which binds a value to a variable or method invocation.

usage.code:
Texture texture = new Texture("texture1.png");
texture.bind();
usage.description: This will bind the texture to the first available sampler slot and apply it to the current active shader program.

items.name: bind

items.description: Specifies which texture slot to use for rendering. It first checks that the input value is within a valid range and then sets the active texture unit using `glActiveTexture()` and binds the specified texture using `glBindTexture()`.

params.name: samplerSlot
params.description: 0-based index of a texture slot in the current active texture unit, with values ranging from 0 to 31, and is used to select the corresponding texture object to bind.

usage.code:
Texture t = new Texture("image.jpg");
t.bind(0);

items.name: bind

items.description: Returns the `id` variable's value.

returns.description: the value of `id`.

items.name: getID

items.description: Loads a texture from a file, creates a ByteBuffer representation of it, and binds it to a GL_TEXTURE_2D texture ID. It also sets texture wrapping and filtering parameters.

params.name: fileName
params.description: name of the texture file to be loaded and read by the `loadTexture()` method.

returns.description: an integer representing the ID of the generated texture.

items.name: loadTexture

structured.description: A `Quaternion` class that represents 4D rotations in homogeneous form. It provides various methods for converting between different representations of quaternions, such as from Euler angles, rotation matrices, and from other quaternions. The code also includes utility methods for calculating quaternion multiplication, conjugation, norm, and inversion. Additionally, it defines a `rotate` method that applies the quaternion rotation to a 3D vector, and a `set` method for setting the quaternion's values directly.
